limitations: APRA CPS 234 does NOT apply to Woolworths Group. Woolworths is a retail conglomerate, not an APRA-regulated entity (no banking licence, superannuation fund, or insurance underwriting licence under Australian prudential law). The assigned regulator for this research task was therefore not the primary applicable regulator. This report re-scoped to the correct applicable regulators: OAIC (Privacy Act) and Department of Home Affairs (Cyber Security Act 2024).

The MyDeal data breach occurred in October 2022 (pre-2024 date rule). However, it is included because: (a) the OAIC regulatory engagement is ongoing and the 2024 Privacy Act reforms have retroactively increased the consequence of such incidents for future enforcement; and (b) the OAIC's 2025 compliance sweep explicitly targets entities with prior breach history — making this a live 2025 regulatory risk signal for Woolworths.

No ISO 27001 or PCI DSS certification was publicly confirmed for Woolworths Group Australia via web search. Woolworths processes payment card transactions at scale and may hold PCI DSS compliance, but no public certification documentation was found. This should be confirmed with the prospect.

The OAIC investigation into the MyDeal breach has not been publicly confirmed as resulting in a formal enforcement action or fine as of the date of this research. The score reflects the confirmed regulatory engagement rather than a concluded enforcement action.

Woolworths' Everyday Insurance business may bring ASIC regulatory obligations into scope (Australian Financial Services Licence), but no specific cybersecurity enforcement actions from ASIC were found for this entity.
APRA.gov.au was searched but CPS 234 applies only to APRA-regulated entities. Woolworths is not in scope for CPS 234. No relevant APRA enforcement actions against Woolworths were found.

sources_checked.notes: CPS 234 confirmed as applying only to APRA-regulated entities (ADIs, insurers, superannuation funds). Woolworths is not an APRA-regulated entity. No relevant findings for Woolworths specifically.
CPS 234 full text reviewed. Confirmed scope is limited to APRA-regulated entities. Woolworths is out of scope.
Cyber Security Act 2024 confirmed in force. Mandatory ransomware reporting applies to Woolworths given its >$3M turnover. Key evidence source.
OAIC confirmed engagement with Woolworths Group over MyDeal NDB scheme obligations. Key evidence of regulatory scrutiny.
OAIC 2025 compliance sweep targeting retailers including entities with prior breach history. Woolworths directly in scope of this sweep.

Woolworths privacy protection page reviewed. References data security measures, hashing/salting, third-party security requirements. No specific security certifications publicly listed.
2024 Annual Report partially retrieved. No explicit cybersecurity certification disclosures found in extracted text. Sustainability and ESG focus confirmed.
New CISO Elrich Engel appointment confirmed (2025). Multi-year technology transformation explicitly referenced. Strong sales signal.

Privacy and Other Legislation Amendment Act 2024 detailed summary. Clarification that 'reasonable steps' now explicitly includes 'technical and organisational measures' — directly relevant to Sonar SAST/DAST.
Confirms Privacy Act 2024 reforms create significantly changed landscape for cyber incident response. In combination with Cyber Security Act 2024, creates compounded regulatory pressure.
Woolworths Group Enterprise Cyber Security Policy summary publicly available. Confirms group-wide policy exists and aligns with global best practices.

snapshot.what_they_do: Woolworths Group is Australia's largest retailer, operating supermarkets, discount department stores (Big W), and B2B foodservice distribution under brands including Woolworths, Woolworths Metro, and PFD Food Services. It serves over 24 million customers per week across more than 1,400 stores in Australia and New Zealand, providing food, everyday needs, and general merchandise.
snapshot.who_they_sell_to: Primarily B2C retail consumers across Australia and New Zealand via supermarkets and online channels. Also serves B2B customers — restaurants, cafes, and institutional buyers — through PFD Food Services, Australia's largest wholesale food distributor. Customers span all demographics, with strong reach to everyday grocery shoppers.
snapshot.regions_scale: Headquartered in Bella Vista, New South Wales, Australia. Operates predominantly in Australia and New Zealand. FY2025 total revenue approximately AUD $69.4 billion (ASX: WOW). Approximately 200,000+ employees across stores, distribution centres, and support offices.
snapshot.business_model: Brick-and-mortar and omnichannel retail (in-store + eCommerce). Revenue primarily from grocery and general merchandise sales; supplemented by B2B wholesale (PFD Food Services), retail media (Cartology), data/analytics services (Quantium), payments (Wpay), and integrated online marketplace commissions (Everyday Market, Big W Market). Not a SaaS company.
snapshot.key_acquisition: MyDeal (2022) — Woolworths acquired an 80% stake in the Australian eCommerce marketplace for approximately AUD $243 million to expand digital and non-grocery retail capabilities. The consumer-facing platform was subsequently closed in September 2025, with its technology absorbed into Woolworths' Big W Market and Everyday Market platforms.
snapshot.strategic_direction: Focusing on core food and everyday needs retail after divesting non-core assets (Endeavour Group demerger completed 2024). Key investments include: eCommerce growth (online sales up ~17% in FY25), automation and supply chain transformation, a $400M cost-cutting program, consolidation of digital marketplaces under owned retail brands, and international brand expansion (rebranding NZ Countdown stores to Woolworths).
snapshot.ai_posture: Actively building and embedding AI into existing products. Key initiatives include: partnering with Google Cloud on a company-wide data platform (with TCS); deploying agentic AI (Google Gemini Enterprise) into its Olive digital shopping assistant — the first Australian retailer on this platform; using AI for personalised promotions, predictive inventory/supply chain, and AI-powered checkout systems. Woolworths has a dedicated internal AI & Machine Learning team.

signal_advisor.hook_title: Australia's Cyber Security Act 2024 Is Live — Woolworths' 24M Weekly Customers Make It a Prime Target and Compliance Obligation
signal_advisor.hook_rationale: The Australian Cyber Security Act 2024 came into force with ransomware payment reporting obligations active from 30 May 2025, and Woolworths — with over 24 million weekly customers, a sprawling multi-cloud codebase (Azure + GCP), and active AI/digital platform development — sits squarely in scope as a high-turnover, high-profile entity. The OAIC has explicitly flagged large retailers as priority targets in its current privacy compliance sweep, compounding the regulatory exposure. Woolworths' February 2026 hiring of Senior Platform Engineers explicitly scoped to DevSecOps and Application Security signals they are actively building out the security function, meaning budget and intent are present — Sonar can land as the code-level SAST layer that closes the application security gap before audit.
signal_advisor.strategy_note: Lead with the regulatory tension: the Australian Cyber Security Act 2024 ransomware reporting obligations went live on 30 May 2025, and the OAIC's current privacy compliance sweep is explicitly targeting large, high-profile retailers — Woolworths by name of sector. The primary hook is the combination of active regulatory enforcement landing on a company with a rapidly expanding digital engineering footprint (multi-cloud, AI-embedded Olive assistant, Everyday Market platform, Wpay payments). The reinforcing signal is the February 2026 hiring push for Senior Platform Engineers with explicit DevSecOps and Application Security mandates, confirming the security investment is active and the wound is open. Frame the tension this way: Woolworths is shipping AI-powered customer-facing features (Gemini-powered Olive, predictive inventory, AI checkout) at pace across a large JavaScript and .NET codebase connected to payment and personal data infrastructure — precisely the attack surface that regulators are scrutinising. Sonar provides the shift-left SAST layer that enforces secure-by-design at the code level via CI/CD quality gates integrated directly into their GitHub pipelines, catching vulnerabilities before they reach the production systems that process 24 million customers' data weekly. Core message: the velocity of Woolworths' AI and platform build-out is expanding the attack surface faster than manual review can cover — Sonar closes that gap at the point of code commit, giving the CISO defensible evidence of proactive security controls under the Cyber Security Act 2024 and OAIC scrutiny. Address Angelo Clayton (CTO) on engineering velocity and developer productivity; address Elrich Engel (CISO) on audit-ready compliance artefacts and vulnerability prevention.

suggested_signals.label: Cyber Security Act 2024 — ransomware reporting live May 2025
suggested_signals.content: The Australian Cyber Security Act 2024 received Royal Assent on 29 November 2024. Mandatory ransomware payment reporting obligations for commercial entities with annual turnover exceeding $3 million took effect from 30 May 2025. Failure to report within 72 hours carries a civil penalty of AUD $99,000 per breach. Woolworths Group, as Australia's largest retailer with annual revenues well above this threshold, is unambiguously in scope. Separately, the Privacy and Other Legislation Amendment Act 2024 introduced revised civil penalties for serious privacy interferences, and the OAIC's current compliance sweep explicitly targets large, high-profile, high-risk retail entities — a description Woolworths fits directly. Together these create an active and escalating compliance obligation around cybersecurity posture and data protection, which traces directly to the security of Woolworths' application code.
suggested_signals.why: Woolworths is directly in scope for live Australian cyber and privacy enforcement, creating immediate compliance pressure that Sonar's SAST and quality gate capabilities directly address.

suggested_signals.label: Feb 2026: Platform Engineers hired for DevSecOps & App Security
suggested_signals.content: Two Senior Platform Engineer roles posted in February 2026 on Woolworths Group's official careers portal (careers.woolworthsgroup.com.au) explicitly list DevSecOps, Application and Hosting Security, CI/CD pipelines, Terraform, and Kubernetes (AKS/GKE) as core competencies. A Security Services Engineer role (cloud-focused, DevSecOps and SIEM) was also identified. Staff Engineer roles in .NET/Full Stack and NodeJS are also currently open, indicating parallel growth in application development headcount. This hiring pattern confirms that Woolworths is actively investing in the intersection of application security and platform engineering — the exact gap Sonar is designed to fill — and that budget and organisational intent are present right now.
suggested_signals.why: Active DevSecOps hiring confirms Woolworths has open budget and mandate for application security tooling, making this an ideal moment to position Sonar as the code-level SAST layer within their GitHub/CI/CD pipeline.

suggested_signals.label: AI-embedded platform expansion: Olive assistant, Wpay, AI checkout
suggested_signals.content: Woolworths is deploying Google Gemini Enterprise agentic AI into its Olive digital shopping assistant, making it the first Australian retailer on this platform. It is also running Wpay (payments), Cartology (retail media), Quantium (data analytics), and Everyday Market (online marketplace) as distinct digital platforms — all of which handle sensitive customer or payment data. The company has a dedicated internal AI & Machine Learning team and is actively expanding its multi-cloud engineering estate across Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud. An ex-Woolworths AI lead spun out in May 2026 to launch a commercial AI decision engine, underlining how deeply AI is embedded in the organisation's technical culture. This rapid AI and platform expansion is widening the application attack surface at speed.
suggested_signals.why: Woolworths is shipping AI-powered, customer-data-handling digital products at pace, creating the exact urgency-plus-gap tension where Sonar's shift-left security gates prevent vulnerable code from reaching production at scale.

tech_stack.summary: Public signals suggest RACQ operates a hybrid multi-cloud environment spanning AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud, confirmed via an ARN article quoting RACQ's infrastructure team. A GitHub organisation at github.com/racqspace (branded racq.space) hosts 15 public repos focused on Ansible automation, Kubernetes, and microk8s, indicating active infrastructure-as-code practice. Third-party listings confirm use of .NET 4.5 and HTML5, and RACQ's ServiceNow case study references a hybrid cloud environment with Microsoft Entra ID integration, indicating a Microsoft-aligned stack. No public evidence of existing Sonar, Snyk, Checkmarx, or other SAST tooling was found.
tech_stack.sonar_relevance_reason: RACQ's confirmed multi-cloud infrastructure (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud), Kubernetes-based container orchestration via the racqspace GitHub org, and .NET/web application stack (.NET 4.5, HTML5, WordPress) represent an active codebase across Sonar-supported languages. The hybrid cloud environment, ServiceNow ITSM platform, and RPA/automation investment signal a mature delivery practice where Sonar quality gates and CI/CD integration would add clear value. No existing SAST tooling is publicly visible, representing a greenfield displacement opportunity.

evidence.name: GitHub Org: racqspace (racq.space)
evidence.evidence_text: A GitHub organisation at github.com/racqspace (titled racq.space) hosts 15 public repositories including ansible-role-awx (install and configure AWX on Kubernetes), ansible-role-microk8s, ansible-role-longhorn (Kubernetes storage), template-playbook-kubernetes, and ansible-role-cert-manager. All repos use YAML and are MIT-licensed. The domain racq.space is consistent with RACQ's brand, though no public members are listed. Repos were last updated in 2021–2023.

evidence.name: .NET 4.5 / HTML5
evidence.evidence_text: Crunchbase (citing G2 Stack) lists RACQ as using 30 technology products including HTML5, WordPress, and .NET 4.5. This signals an active web application stack with at least one confirmed Microsoft .NET language-family technology.

evidence.name: Kubernetes / microk8s
evidence.evidence_text: The racqspace GitHub org includes ansible-role-microk8s ('install and configure microk8s on Ubuntu') and template-playbook-kubernetes, along with Longhorn storage configuration for Kubernetes clusters. This indicates hands-on Kubernetes infrastructure management practice.

evidence.name: AWS / Azure / Google Cloud (multi-cloud)
evidence.evidence_text: An ARN article quotes RACQ's infrastructure lead: 'we're currently developing a common API so we can provision wherever we need to, whether that's on Nutanix, AWS, Azure, or Google.' This directly confirms RACQ's use of AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud as part of a multi-cloud strategy, validated during a 2019 data centre migration project.

evidence.name: Amazon EC2 (AWS)
evidence.evidence_text: AppsRunTheWorld identifies RACQ as having purchased Amazon EC2 for Application Hosting and Computing Services in 2017, corroborating the ARN multi-cloud reference.

evidence.name: Microsoft Azure / Hybrid Cloud (Microsoft Entra ID)
evidence.evidence_text: ServiceNow's RACQ case study states: 'Having a hybrid cloud environment, RACQ uses ServiceNow ITSM with ServiceNow Workflow Data Fabric spokes for Microsoft Entra ID to sync back to on-prem AD and UiPath for automation.' This confirms Azure Active Directory / Entra ID as part of RACQ's identity infrastructure.

evidence.name: UiPath RPA / Automation Centre of Excellence
evidence.evidence_text: UiPath published a case study naming RACQ's automation journey, referencing a makeshift centre of excellence for RPA development and production, and an Automation Technical Lead role. This confirms active software delivery and automation engineering at RACQ.

evidence.name: New Relic APM
evidence.evidence_text: AppsRunTheWorld identifies RACQ as a New Relic APM (Application Performance Management) customer since 2013, indicating application observability tooling is in place.

limitations: The racqspace GitHub org is branded 'racq.space' (not racq.com.au) and has no public members listed. Attribution to RACQ (Royal Automobile Club of Queensland) is inferred from naming consistency but not confirmed via an official RACQ statement or linked domain.
No public evidence found for specific CI/CD tools (GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps Pipelines, Jenkins, etc.) explicitly linked to RACQ's software delivery pipeline.
No public evidence of any SAST, code quality, or security scanning tooling (SonarQube, SonarCloud, Snyk, Checkmarx, Veracode, etc.) in use at RACQ.
The .NET 4.5 signal from Crunchbase/G2 Stack is from a third-party aggregator and may reflect legacy web stack rather than active application development languages.
RACQ's primary GitHub activity in the racqspace org was last updated in 2021–2023, so infra tooling may have evolved.
No engineering blog or developer documentation found on racq.com.au confirming specific programming languages used in application development.
The tech stack listing from RocketReach (259 technologies) is marketing/analytics tool focused and does not confirm software development languages.
Private repositories, internal tooling, and proprietary development environments are not visible from public sources.

hiring_patterns.summary: RACQ is actively hiring across technology and compliance functions in Brisbane, with confirmed open roles including a DevOps Engineer, Data Engineer, Junior Integration Developer (with explicit OWASP Top 10 and secure coding requirements), Manager Compliance Advisory, and Business Risk Officer — all posted within the last 7 days. The Junior Integration Developer posting explicitly references OWASP Top 10 and secure coding principles as part of the role's responsibilities, indicating security awareness being embedded into their SDLC. RACQ uses Taleo as its ATS (hosted on racq.com.au) and was actively maintaining its careers infrastructure as recently as June 15–24, 2026, with Taleo undergoing planned maintenance.
hiring_patterns.sonar_relevance_reason: The confirmed DevOps Engineer hire signals active CI/CD pipeline investment, a key Sonar entry point for quality gate integration. The Junior Integration Developer posting's explicit reference to OWASP Top 10 and secure coding principles indicates RACQ is beginning to embed security awareness into software delivery — exactly the gap Sonar fills with SAST and SCA capabilities in the pipeline. As a financial services and insurance organisation with 1.7M members, RACQ operates under Australian financial regulation (APRA CPS 234), which creates compliance-driven demand for code security tooling. The concurrent compliance and risk hiring (Manager Compliance Advisory, Business Risk Officer) further signals a regulated-environment security posture uplift.

regulatory_impact.industry_detected: Automotive & Financial Services (Banking, General Insurance, Motoring Club)

applicable_regulations.name: APRA CPS 234 Information Security
applicable_regulations.reason: RACQ Insurance Limited and RACQ Bank (Members Banking Group Limited) are both APRA-regulated entities and are directly subject to CPS 234. The standard mandates information security capability, systematic control testing (including penetration testing and vulnerability assessments), secure software development and acquisition, and annual testing of information security response plans. APRA has an ongoing heightened supervisory focus on CPS 234 compliance across all banks and insurers, with active industry letters issued in June 2024 and August 2024 identifying common cyber control weaknesses.

applicable_regulations.name: APRA CPS 230 Operational Risk Management
applicable_regulations.reason: CPS 230 came into force on 1 July 2025 and applies to RACQ as an authorised deposit-taking institution (RACQ Bank) and general insurer (RACQ Insurance). It explicitly requires entities to maintain sound IT capability, manage technology risk, and maintain information security in line with CPS 234. APRA has signalled active compliance monitoring and enforcement from 2025 onward.

applicable_regulations.name: APRA Risk Transformation Enforcement (RACQ-specific, ongoing)
applicable_regulations.reason: APRA took formal enforcement action against RACQ Insurance and RACQ Bank requiring a comprehensive APRA-approved risk transformation program, citing significant weaknesses in risk and compliance framework and practices. This program was still being monitored by APRA in its 2023-24 Corporate Plan. RACQ subsequently appointed a new CIO in November 2024 with explicit responsibility for cybersecurity, indicating the transformation is ongoing.

applicable_regulations.name: APRA CPG 234 Guidance — Secure Software Development
applicable_regulations.reason: The APRA Prudential Practice Guide CPG 234 explicitly addresses secure software development and acquisition as an outcome required of APRA-regulated entities. This provides a direct regulatory hook for Sonar's SAST and quality gate capabilities in RACQ's development lifecycle.

regulatory_impact.summary: RACQ (comprising RACQ Insurance Limited and RACQ Bank / Members Banking Group Limited) is a directly APRA-regulated entity subject to CPS 234 Information Security and the newly commenced CPS 230 Operational Risk Management (July 2025). APRA has previously taken formal enforcement action against RACQ requiring a risk transformation program due to significant weaknesses in risk governance, risk and compliance framework practices, and an immature risk culture. RACQ publicly committed to significant investment in its systems and processes as part of this remediation. RACQ's new CIO (appointed November 2024) holds explicit responsibility for cybersecurity. APRA's June 2024 and August 2024 industry letters identified common weaknesses in security testing, configuration management, and privileged access management across all regulated entities including banks and insurers — all areas directly in scope for RACQ. CPS 234 mandates systematic control testing including penetration testing and vulnerability assessments, and CPG 234 explicitly references secure software development and acquisition as a required outcome.
regulatory_impact.sonar_relevance_reason: RACQ is under active APRA regulatory pressure on multiple fronts: (1) an ongoing risk transformation program stemming from a formal APRA enforcement action citing risk and compliance framework weaknesses; (2) mandatory CPS 234 compliance requiring systematic information security control testing including vulnerability assessments and penetration testing; (3) CPG 234 guidance explicitly referencing secure software development as a required outcome; (4) CPS 230 operational resilience requirements (live from July 2025) mandating technology risk management and IT capability; and (5) APRA's active industry-wide supervisory focus on security testing gaps in banking and insurance. Sonar's SAST, DAST, and quality gates directly address the secure SDLC and vulnerability management obligations these regulations mandate, and would strengthen the audit-ready evidence trail APRA expects to see in control effectiveness testing.
regulatory_impact.recommended_sales_angle: Lead with the APRA enforcement action and risk transformation program: RACQ has already been publicly cited by APRA for risk and compliance framework weaknesses and is mid-transformation. Their new CIO (November 2024) owns cybersecurity — making this a timely executive conversation. Frame Sonar as the tool that operationalises the secure software development outcomes required by CPG 234 and the systematic control testing required by CPS 234 paragraph 27. Specifically: (1) Sonar's SAST and quality gates provide the audit-ready evidence trail APRA expects for information security control effectiveness; (2) Sonar catches vulnerabilities before production, directly addressing APRA's August 2024 finding of 'inadequate testing coverage'; (3) CPS 230 now adds technology risk and IT capability as an explicit operational resilience obligation — Sonar embeds security into the SDLC rather than bolting it on post-deployment. Urgency: CPS 230 is live from July 2025, APRA has signalled increased enforcement, and RACQ's transformation program requires independent assurance — Sonar strengthens that assurance posture.

evidence.value: APRA CPS 234 Information Security — mandatory for RACQ as a general insurer and authorised deposit-taking institution; requires systematic control testing including penetration testing and vulnerability assessments, secure software development and acquisition, and annual testing of information security response plans.
evidence.evidence_text: An APRA-regulated entity must test the effectiveness of its information security controls through a systematic testing program. The nature and frequency of the systematic testing must be commensurate with: (a) the rate at which the vulnerabilities and threats change; (b) the criticality and sensitivity of the information asset; (c) the consequences of an information security incident.

evidence.value: APRA August 2024 industry letter on common cyber resilience weaknesses — specifically identifies security testing gaps across banking, superannuation, and insurance; directly applicable to RACQ as a bank and insurer. APRA expects all regulated entities to review their control environment and address gaps notifiable under CPS 234 paragraph 36.
evidence.evidence_text: This letter aims to share additional insights and guidance related to the common cyber weaknesses observed in terms of security in configuration management, privileged access management and security testing. APRA expects regulated entities to review their control environment against these common weaknesses.

evidence.value: APRA CPG 234 Information Security Practice Guide — explicitly references secure software development and acquisition as a required outcome for APRA-regulated entities. Directly maps to Sonar's SAST and secure SDLC capabilities.
evidence.evidence_text: The outcome of secure software development and acquisition is to ensure that software: (a) continues to function as intended regardless of unforeseen circumstances.

evidence.value: APRA CPS 230 Operational Risk Management — commenced 1 July 2025, applies to RACQ Bank and RACQ Insurance. Explicitly requires entities to manage technology risk and maintain sound IT capability, with CPS 234 information security requirements embedded within it.
evidence.evidence_text: An APRA-regulated entity must maintain appropriate and sound information and information technology (IT) capability to meet its current and projected business requirements and to support its critical operations and risk management. In managing technology risks, an APRA-regulated entity must monitor the age and health of its information assets and meet the requirements for information security.

evidence.value: APRA 2024-25 Corporate Plan — cyber resilience named as strategic priority; APRA will monitor banks and insurers to ensure CPS 234 compliance, may intensify supervision, require root cause analysis, request remediation plans, and consider enforcement action.
evidence.evidence_text: Strengthening cyber security across our regulated industries remaining a top priority in APRA's 2025-26 Corporate Plan, APRA will continue to keep a strong focus on ensuring banks, insurers and super funds stay on top of this constantly evolving threat.
evidence.regulation: CPS 234 Information Security; CPS 230 Operational Risk Management

snapshot.what_they_do: RACQ (Royal Automobile Club of Queensland) is a member-owned mutual organisation founded in 1905 that provides roadside assistance, motoring services, insurance (distributed under a 25-year alliance with IAG), banking, travel, and solar energy products to Queenslanders. Its purpose is to help members live and move safely, securely, and sustainably across Queensland.
snapshot.who_they_sell_to: RACQ serves individual consumers (B2C) in Queensland and northern New South Wales, with a membership base of over 1.7 million members. It also offers business membership benefits. Its primary market is Queensland residents seeking motoring, insurance, financial, and energy services.
snapshot.regions_scale: RACQ is headquartered in Eight Mile Plains, Brisbane, Queensland, and operates across 50+ locations statewide. It employs approximately 1,000–2,200 staff (various estimates; post-insurance and banking divestitures headcount is in flux). Total revenue was approximately AUD $2.6 billion in FY2025 per IBISWorld, though much of this reflects the now-divested insurance underwriting business.
snapshot.business_model: Membership-based mutual organisation (not publicly listed). Revenue is generated through annual membership subscriptions, roadside assistance plans, insurance distribution commissions (now via the IAG alliance), banking referral arrangements (post-Bendigo Bank sale), solar energy products, and travel services. The model is shifting from underwriting and direct banking toward a distribution and referral partnership model.
snapshot.key_acquisition: No major acquisitions by RACQ in the last 3 years. Notably, RACQ executed significant divestitures: it sold 90% of RACQ Insurance to IAG for AUD $855 million (completed September 2025) under a 25-year exclusive distribution alliance, and announced the sale of its retail banking loan and deposit book (~AUD $5.2 billion in assets) to Bendigo Bank (announced December 2025, completion expected H2 2026).
snapshot.strategic_direction: RACQ is undergoing a major strategic pivot — divesting its insurance underwriting and retail banking businesses to focus on its core membership, roadside assistance, motoring services, solar/EV, and digital member experience. It is investing heavily in digital transformation and advancing its 2032 Strategy, which includes developing new products around home and mobility, EV infrastructure (supporting 132 public EV charging sites), and accelerating digital capabilities via strategic partnerships.
snapshot.ai_posture: Actively embedding AI into member experience and digital operations. In June 2026, RACQ signed a landmark 5-year 'Lighthouse' strategic partnership with Adobe and Deloitte Digital — the first of its kind in Asia Pacific and Japan — to deploy Adobe's agentic, generative, and predictive AI capabilities for real-time personalisation, scalable content production, and intelligent automation. RACQ gains early access to Adobe's new AI tools including Adobe Experience Platform Agent Orchestrator and Adobe Real-Time CDP Collaboration.

signal_advisor.hook_title: RACQ's Adobe AI Acceleration Meets APRA CPS 234's Secure Software Development Mandate
signal_advisor.hook_rationale: RACQ signed a landmark 5-year agentic AI partnership with Adobe and Deloitte Digital in June 2026 — the first of its kind in Asia Pacific — meaning their engineering and integration teams are about to ship AI-embedded features at materially higher velocity. APRA CPS 234 explicitly requires systematic security testing commensurate with the rate at which vulnerabilities and threats change, and CPG 234 mandates secure software development outcomes. Their Junior Integration Developer posting explicitly calls out OWASP Top 10 and secure coding, confirming the gap between stated intent and systematic enforcement is open right now.
signal_advisor.strategy_note: Lead with the tension between RACQ's June 2026 Adobe AI 'Lighthouse' partnership and their APRA CPS 234 / CPG 234 obligations. The primary hook is regulatory: CPS 234 requires security testing frequency to be commensurate with the rate of change in vulnerabilities and threats, and CPG 234 mandates that software continues to function securely regardless of unforeseen circumstances — both of which become harder to satisfy as AI-driven development accelerates. Reinforce with the Junior Integration Developer posting that explicitly cites OWASP Top 10 and secure coding, which shows RACQ knows the problem but is trying to solve it through hiring rather than systematic tooling. Sonar addresses this directly: automated SAST at every code commit enforces OWASP Top 10 coverage and secure coding standards without relying on individual developer knowledge, and quality gates in CI/CD provide the auditable, systematic testing evidence APRA expects. Also flag CPS 230 — it requires sound IT capability to manage technology risk, and a manually-enforced security posture scaling into an AI build-out is a documented risk. Core message: the Adobe AI partnership is accelerating the rate of code change at RACQ at exactly the moment APRA requires security testing to keep pace — Sonar provides the systematic, auditable secure-by-design enforcement that makes the compliance posture defensible. Address to Ben Johnston (CTO, Blue/Red — lead with risk evidence and data) with a CC or follow-up angle to Anna Clive (Engineering Director, Yellow/Green — lead with developer enablement and team productivity).

suggested_signals.label: APRA CPS 234 — systematic security testing at pace of change
suggested_signals.content: RACQ is an APRA-regulated entity subject to CPS 234 Information Security, which mandates that systematic security testing frequency must be 'commensurate with the rate at which vulnerabilities and threats change' and the criticality of information assets. CPG 234 further specifies that secure software development must ensure software 'continues to function as intended regardless of unforeseen circumstances.' APRA has also published additional guidance flagging security testing and configuration management as the most common cyber resilience weaknesses observed in regulated entities. As RACQ scales AI-driven digital capabilities, the rate of code and configuration change increases materially — making the compliance obligation to demonstrate systematic, auditable security testing more acute, not less.
suggested_signals.why: CPS 234's explicit link between testing frequency and rate of change creates a direct, auditable compliance gap for any regulated entity accelerating its software delivery — this is a named regulatory obligation Sonar can directly address.

suggested_signals.label: June 2026: RACQ signs landmark Adobe AI 'Lighthouse' partnership
suggested_signals.content: In June 2026, RACQ signed a 5-year strategic 'Lighthouse' partnership with Adobe and Deloitte Digital — described as the first of its kind in Asia Pacific and Japan — to deploy agentic, generative, and predictive AI capabilities across their member experience and digital operations. RACQ gains early access to Adobe Experience Platform Agent Orchestrator and Adobe Real-Time CDP Collaboration. This means RACQ engineering and integration teams are actively building and integrating AI-driven features at accelerated pace, increasing the volume and complexity of code changes across their digital estate.
suggested_signals.why: An AI build-out of this scale and pace is the exact trigger that widens the gap between current security testing practices and the CPS 234 obligation to test at the rate of change — creating immediate urgency for Sonar.

suggested_signals.label: Junior Integration Developer — OWASP Top 10 & secure coding required
suggested_signals.content: RACQ posted a Junior Integration Developer role (LinkedIn, within the last 7 days) that explicitly lists OWASP Top 10 and secure coding principles as part of the role's responsibilities. This is a significant signal: it confirms RACQ is aware of application security requirements and is attempting to embed them into their SDLC — but is doing so through individual hiring rather than systematic tooling. A junior developer expected to manually enforce OWASP Top 10 in a .NET/C# environment scaling into an AI partnership is not a scalable or auditable compliance posture. RACQ is also hiring a DevOps Engineer, Manager Compliance Advisory, and Business Risk Officer simultaneously, reinforcing that risk and compliance resourcing is a current priority.
suggested_signals.why: Explicitly naming OWASP Top 10 in a job description confirms RACQ sees the gap and is trying to fill it with headcount — Sonar reframes this as a tooling problem that headcount alone cannot solve at the pace of their AI build-out.

suggested_signals.label: CPS 230 — sound IT capability to manage technology risk at scale
suggested_signals.content: CPS 230 Operational Risk Management requires RACQ to maintain 'appropriate and sound information and IT capability to meet its current and projected business requirements' and to monitor and manage technology risks. With a major AI partnership expanding their projected business requirements and digital surface area, a manually-enforced or developer-dependent security posture represents a documented technology risk gap under CPS 230. Regulators expect entities to demonstrate proactive risk management in their IT controls, not reactive patching.
suggested_signals.why: CPS 230 adds a second regulatory vector — operational risk management — that makes the case for systematic code security tooling a business continuity and governance argument, not just a security one.

tech_stack.summary: Public signals suggest Harvey Norman maintains an active in-house software development team building and supporting custom applications in C# / .NET and Java (J2EE), with hiring data evidencing ongoing development activity across multiple roles. The company uses Atlassian Bamboo for CI/CD and operates workloads on Microsoft Azure cloud. No public evidence of SonarQube, SonarCloud, or competing SAST/code-quality tooling was found.
tech_stack.sonar_relevance_reason: Harvey Norman has a confirmed in-house C# / .NET and Java codebase, an active Bamboo CI/CD pipeline, and Microsoft Azure infrastructure — all environments where Sonar integrates natively. The absence of any detected code quality or SAST tooling represents a clear greenfield opportunity for SonarQube or SonarCloud adoption.

public_news.summary: Public reports suggest Harvey Norman is actively expanding its retail footprint, with the launch of a fourth UK store within the past 90 days. This physical expansion likely signals accompanying digital and platform investment to support new locations, creating a potential opening for Sonar to engage around code quality and security for any supporting e-commerce or retail systems being built or scaled. The recency of these signals — including a competitor launch just 7 days ago putting pressure on Harvey Norman's homeware market position — adds urgency to the conversation.
limitations: No technology, engineering, or security news about Harvey Norman (harveynorman.com.au) was found published after 2026-03-26 across all six search queries.

company_position.summary: Public signals suggest Harvey Norman has a reasonably mature technology foundation, earning an AI Leader classification at medium confidence with a score of 6 out of 10. The strongest evidence comes from tech stack maturity, where signals indicate established CI/CD tooling (Bamboo), cloud infrastructure across Microsoft Azure and Rackspace, and use of C# / .NET and Java — pointing to an organisation that has invested meaningfully in modern engineering practices. Hiring signals and public engineering visibility (GitHub presence, DevOps and software engineering roles) further support this positioning, though the absence of any leadership AI messaging means the classification should be treated as directional rather than definitive.
company_position.classification_reason: The AI Leader label was assigned primarily on the strength of a mature, cloud-connected tech stack with CI/CD tooling and multi-cloud infrastructure, supported by engineering visibility and active technical hiring — despite no detectable public AI leadership messaging from executives.
company_position.recommended_sales_angle: Given Harvey Norman's established CI/CD pipeline and cloud infrastructure, Sonar is well positioned as the code quality and security layer that helps their engineering teams ship with confidence at scale. As their DevOps and software engineering headcount grows, automated quality gates and security scanning become critical to maintaining code health across distributed teams working in C# / .NET and Java codebases. Frame Sonar as the foundation that lets Harvey Norman's engineering organisation move faster without accumulating technical debt or security risk — particularly relevant as AI-assisted coding tools may increasingly enter their development workflow.
limitations: Confidence is Medium — this classification is directional and should not be treated as a definitive assessment of Harvey Norman's internal technology strategy.
Leadership AI messaging score is zero, meaning there are no public signals of AI strategy or vision from executives, which is a notable gap for an AI Leader classification.
Evidence is limited to six items, and no skeptic flags were raised — absence of cautious signals does not confirm strong AI enthusiasm.
Harvey Norman is primarily a retail organisation; public engineering signals may reflect e-commerce or internal IT functions rather than a broad engineering culture.
Tech stack signals (Bamboo, Azure, Rackspace) indicate tooling presence but do not confirm active, modern usage or maturity of DevSecOps practices.
Internal strategy, AI adoption plans, and actual development practices may differ significantly from what is publicly visible.

regulatory_impact.regulator_checked: APRA (Australian Prudential Regulation Authority) — confirmed as not the primary regulator for Harvey Norman; primary applicable bodies are the OAIC, ASD/Home Affairs (Cyber Security Act 2024), and PCI SSC

applicable_regulations.name: PCI DSS v4.0.1 (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard)
applicable_regulations.reason: Harvey Norman is one of Australia's largest retail chains processing very high volumes of payment card transactions across physical stores and e-commerce. PCI DSS v4.0.1 is now the only active version (v3.2.1 retired March 2024). All 51 future-dated requirements — including Requirement 6 mandating application security controls and software vulnerability management — became fully mandatory on 31 March 2025. Requirement 6.2 specifically mandates bespoke and custom software be developed securely, directly implicating secure SDLC and SAST tooling. As a high-volume merchant, Harvey Norman is likely assessed as a Level 1 or Level 2 merchant requiring annual audits or self-assessments and quarterly vulnerability scans.

applicable_regulations.name: Australia Cyber Security Act 2024 (Cth)
applicable_regulations.reason: Australia's Cyber Security Act 2024 received Royal Assent on 29 November 2024 and the mandatory ransomware reporting regime took effect 29 May 2025. Any entity with annual turnover exceeding $3 million — Harvey Norman's FY2025 revenue was AU$2.92 billion — must report ransomware payments to the Australian Signals Directorate within 72 hours. This creates board-level urgency around cyber incident preparedness and software vulnerability controls to prevent ransomware entry points.

applicable_regulations.name: Privacy and Other Legislation Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) — Privacy Act Reforms
applicable_regulations.reason: Australia's privacy law reforms passed in November 2024 and took effect from June 2025, introducing a statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy, expanded OAIC enforcement powers, and fines of up to $50 million for serious data breaches. Harvey Norman holds extensive customer data across its retail, franchise, and e-commerce operations. The reforms implicitly require organisations to demonstrate secure data handling within live software systems, reinforcing pressure on secure SDLC and application security practices.

applicable_regulations.name: APRA CPS 234 Information Security (indirect third-party exposure)
applicable_regulations.reason: APRA CPS 234 applies exclusively to APRA-regulated entities (banks, insurers, superannuation funds) and does NOT directly apply to Harvey Norman as a retail company. However, Harvey Norman's finance partner Latitude Financial is APRA-regulated, and Latitude suffered a major 2023 data breach affecting Harvey Norman customers. CPS 234's third-party obligations mean that if Harvey Norman manages data assets on behalf of any APRA-regulated entity, it may be indirectly subject to CPS 234 requirements. The OAIC and NZ Privacy Commissioner launched a joint investigation into the Latitude breach, with ongoing regulatory scrutiny that touches Harvey Norman's ecosystem.

regulatory_impact.summary: Harvey Norman is Australia's largest consumer electronics and home goods retailer with FY2025 revenues of AU$2.92 billion, operating across eight countries. As a high-volume payment card merchant, Harvey Norman is subject to PCI DSS v4.0.1 — now fully enforced from March 2025, including Requirement 6 which mandates application security controls, secure software development practices, and vulnerability management in bespoke and custom software. Australia's Cyber Security Act 2024, in force since May 2025, requires Harvey Norman (well above the $3M turnover threshold) to report ransomware payments to the Australian Signals Directorate within 72 hours — creating board-level urgency around reducing attack surface through secure software. Australia's Privacy Act reforms (June 2025) impose higher penalties (up to $50M) and expanded OAIC enforcement powers. Harvey Norman's finance ecosystem partner, Latitude Financial, suffered a catastrophic 2023 data breach affecting millions of Harvey Norman customers, triggering a joint OAIC/New Zealand Privacy Commissioner investigation — underscoring the cyber risk sensitivity of Harvey Norman's partner and customer data environment. APRA CPS 234 does not directly apply to Harvey Norman as it is not an APRA-regulated entity, but indirect exposure exists through its finance partners. No direct enforcement action against Harvey Norman itself has been identified in 2024–2025.
regulatory_impact.sonar_relevance_reason: PCI DSS v4.0.1 Requirement 6 directly mandates secure software development and vulnerability management for Harvey Norman's custom retail and e-commerce software — Sonar's SAST, DAST, and quality gates directly address these requirements. The Cyber Security Act 2024's ransomware reporting obligation creates board-level urgency to reduce software vulnerabilities that enable ransomware entry. The Latitude breach and ongoing OAIC scrutiny of Harvey Norman's broader customer data ecosystem amplifies the reputational stakes of a software-related security incident. Australia's record 1,113 data breach notifications in 2024 and the retail sector's demonstrated exposure to credential stuffing and ransomware attacks reinforce the urgency of proactive secure SDLC controls.
regulatory_impact.recommended_sales_angle: Lead with PCI DSS v4.0.1 Requirement 6: since 31 March 2025, all 51 formerly future-dated requirements are now fully mandatory — including Req 6.2 (bespoke software must be developed securely) and Req 6.3 (security vulnerabilities must be identified and addressed). Position Sonar's SAST and quality gates as the audit-ready evidence trail for PCI DSS Requirement 6 compliance in Harvey Norman's e-commerce and internal software pipelines. Secondary angle: tie Sonar to Australia's Cyber Security Act 2024 ransomware preparedness — secure code reduces the attack surface that ransomware exploits, and demonstrable SDLC controls support the board's 72-hour reporting readiness. Reference the Latitude breach as a cautionary third-party data risk example to reinforce that vulnerability in any part of the partner ecosystem — including software — can create regulatory and reputational exposure for Harvey Norman.

limitations: APRA CPS 234 is confirmed as NOT directly applicable to Harvey Norman — it applies only to APRA-regulated financial entities. The assigned regulator (APRA) is not the primary regulatory authority for a retail company. The actual primary regulators are the OAIC (Privacy Act), ASD/Home Affairs (Cyber Security Act 2024), and the PCI SSC/acquiring banks (PCI DSS).

Harvey Norman does not publicly disclose a security trust center, ISO 27001 certification, SOC 2 report, or PCI DSS compliance status. No evidence of public certifications could be confirmed from their website.
The Latitude Financial breach (2023) involved Harvey Norman's finance partner, not Harvey Norman directly — no direct regulatory action against Harvey Norman itself has been identified in 2024–2025.

Harvey Norman's PCI DSS merchant level classification (Level 1, 2, 3, or 4) could not be confirmed from public sources, though their transaction volumes strongly suggest Level 1 or Level 2 obligations.
The OAIC investigation into Latitude Financial remains ongoing as of early 2024 with no public conclusion reported — the indirect impact on Harvey Norman cannot be fully quantified.

No Harvey Norman-specific cybersecurity audit reports, IT governance disclosures, or security program details were found in their FY2024 Corporate Governance Statement or annual report via public search.

snapshot.what_they_do: Harvey Norman Holdings Limited is an ASX-listed integrated retail, franchise, property, and digital enterprise that sells consumer electronics, furniture, bedding, appliances, and home goods. It operates via a franchise model in Australia and directly owned stores internationally, solving consumers' home and lifestyle product needs across physical and digital channels.
snapshot.who_they_sell_to: Primarily B2C retail consumers across Australia and internationally, spanning home and lifestyle categories (electronics, furniture, appliances, bedding). Serves broad consumer demographics across Oceania, Southeast Asia, and Europe — not vertically specialised. No significant enterprise or SMB software/services customer base.
snapshot.regions_scale: 316 total company-owned and franchised stores as of 2025, spanning Australia, New Zealand, Ireland, Northern Ireland, Singapore, Malaysia, Slovenia, Croatia, and the UK. Over 10,000 employees. FY2025 total system sales revenue of AUD 9.35 billion; consolidated revenue of AUD 4.47 billion; profit after tax of AUD 518 million (up 47% YoY). Total assets exceed AUD 8.37 billion.
snapshot.business_model: Hybrid franchise and direct ownership retail model. In Australia, ~195 franchised complexes pay lease fees and a share of sales revenue to Harvey Norman Holdings. Overseas (~121 stores), stores are directly owned and operated. Additional revenue streams include a substantial freehold property portfolio (AUD 4.53 billion), consumer finance, extended warranties, and e-commerce. Not a SaaS or software business.
snapshot.strategic_direction: Harvey Norman is pursuing active international expansion (new stores in the UK, Malaysia, New Zealand, Singapore, and Croatia in FY25), omnichannel investment (click-and-collect, real-time inventory, online-to-offline integration), and positioning itself as a leading retailer of AI-enabled consumer devices including AI PCs, smart home tech, and next-gen appliances. The company is also investing in digital customer experience tools such as Freshworks and Algolia's search platform, alongside continued cybersecurity investment.

The company is stocking and promoting AI-enabled technology from brands such as Acer, Asus, and Lenovo, and has deployed AI-driven inventory forecasting internally. AI is a sales tailwind, not a core R&D or software investment.

signal_advisor.hook_title: PCI DSS v4.0.1 Application Security Mandates Land on Harvey Norman's Payment-Processing Codebase
signal_advisor.hook_rationale: Harvey Norman processes payments at scale across ~316 stores and an e-commerce platform, making PCI DSS v4.0.1 directly applicable — and the 51 future-dated requirements that became fully mandatory on 31 March 2025 include expanded web-facing application security controls (Requirement 6) that Harvey Norman's C#/.NET and Java codebase must now satisfy. This is a live, enforceable compliance obligation with real consequences for a retailer of this size, not a theoretical risk. The concurrent hiring of multiple Cyber Security Analysts into a 'growing Cyber Security team' signals that Harvey Norman is actively investing in closing security gaps right now, making this the optimal moment for Sonar to enter the conversation.
signal_advisor.strategy_note: Lead with the PCI DSS v4.0.1 enforcement reality: the 51 previously future-dated requirements — including expanded Requirement 6 controls mandating security testing integrated into the software development lifecycle — became fully mandatory on 31 March 2025. Harvey Norman's payment-processing web applications and internal retail systems, built on C#/.NET and Java and delivered via Bamboo CI/CD pipelines, are directly in scope. The tension is this: Harvey Norman is visibly investing in its security team (multiple Cyber Security Analyst hires, an Information Security Assurance role, all in a self-described 'growing Cyber Security team' at Homebush West HQ in 2026) but PCI DSS Requirement 6 specifically demands that security is embedded at the code level — something a growing SOC or network security team cannot deliver on its own. Sonar closes that gap by providing SAST integrated directly into Bamboo pipelines, catching vulnerabilities in C#/.NET and Java before code reaches production, and generating the audit-ready artefacts that demonstrate Requirement 6 compliance. Core message: Harvey Norman's security team is growing to meet PCI DSS obligations, but compliance at the code level requires developer-side tooling — Sonar is what makes Requirement 6 provable in audit. Address outreach to Gary Wheelhouse (Chief Digital Officer) given his ownership of digital/e-commerce delivery, and copy or reference Gordon Dingwall (CIO) for infrastructure and compliance framing. Do not open with generic security language — anchor immediately to PCI DSS v4.0.1 Requirement 6 and the March 2025 enforcement date as the specific, time-stamped event that makes this urgent.

suggested_signals.label: PCI DSS v4.0.1 Req 6 — mandatory since March 2025
suggested_signals.content: PCI DSS v4.0.1 is now fully enforced, with 51 previously future-dated requirements having become mandatory on 31 March 2025. Requirement 6 specifically mandates that organisations develop and maintain secure systems and software, including integrating security testing into the software development lifecycle for web-facing applications. Harvey Norman processes card payments across hundreds of physical stores and an e-commerce platform, placing its C#/.NET and Java applications squarely in scope. The Australian cybersecurity landscape adds further urgency: the OAIC received over 500 data breach notifications between January and June 2024, with payment card data among the most targeted, and Australian organisations must satisfy both PCI DSS and local Privacy Act obligations. Non-compliance exposes Harvey Norman to card scheme fines, potential suspension of card processing rights, and reputational damage in a highly competitive retail environment.
suggested_signals.why: PCI DSS Requirement 6 creates a direct, enforceable mandate for application-layer security controls that Sonar's SAST capability satisfies — making this an immediately actionable compliance hook tied to Harvey Norman's core payment infrastructure.

An Integration Testing/QA Analyst role focused on 'key systems that power the business' was also posted in June 2026. This hiring pattern indicates Harvey Norman is investing in security capability right now — but the roles are oriented toward network, infrastructure, and assurance functions rather than application security or DevSecOps, leaving a gap at the code level that Sonar addresses.
suggested_signals.why: Active security hiring confirms budget and organisational momentum around security, but the absence of AppSec or DevSecOps roles reveals exactly the gap Sonar fills — giving the sales rep a precise, non-generic angle for outreach.

tech_stack.summary: Public signals confirm that the NSW Department of Education operates an active internal software engineering function, with a Principal Web Developer job posting explicitly naming Java, NodeJS, JavaScript, and AWS as core technologies for building RESTful APIs and AEM-based web platforms. The department has formally adopted Adobe Experience Manager (a Java/OSGi-based platform) for managing over 2,200 school websites, and has a public GitHub organisation (govnsw-doe) with at least one HTML repository. No public evidence was found of CI/CD tooling, security scanning tools, or containerisation in use internally.
tech_stack.sonar_relevance_reason: Java and JavaScript/NodeJS are confirmed languages in the department's internal development stack via a public job posting, both of which are first-class Sonar-supported languages. The AEM/Adobe Experience Manager platform is Java-based (OSGi/Sling), making Sonar directly applicable for static analysis. AWS cloud usage and a large-scale web platform suggest delivery complexity and a codebase where code quality and security gates would add measurable value. The absence of any detected security tooling represents a greenfield displacement opportunity.

While there is some evidence of foundational technology use — including AWS cloud infrastructure and standard web development languages — no signals were detected around AI innovation, engineering leadership messaging, or a strong hiring push for advanced technical roles. The strongest evidence dimension is tech stack maturity, which reflects a basic but established technology foundation rather than a forward-leaning engineering culture.

regulatory_impact.regulator_checked: Cyber Security NSW (Department of Customer Service) — Note: APRA does not regulate this entity. The applicable regulators are Cyber Security NSW and the NSW Audit Office.

applicable_regulations.name: NSW Cyber Security Policy (CSP) 2023-2024 — Mandatory for all NSW Government Agencies
applicable_regulations.reason: The NSW CSP is mandatory for all NSW Government departments and agencies, including the Department of Education NSW. It mandates 31 Mandatory Requirements across three domains — Govern & Identify, Detect/Respond/Recover, and Protect — including vulnerability management (MR 1.11), application patching (MR 3.3), application control (MR 3.6), user application hardening (MR 3.8), and annual compliance attestation to Cyber Security NSW. The department has publicly confirmed its obligations under this policy.

applicable_regulations.name: ACSC Essential Eight (Maturity Level 1 — Mandatory under NSW CSP)
applicable_regulations.reason: The NSW CSP mandates implementation of the ACSC Essential Eight at a minimum of Maturity Level 1 across all applicable ICT environments. This includes application control, application patching, and user application hardening — all of which have direct software security and vulnerability management implications. The Department of Education NSW must annually report its Essential Eight maturity level to Cyber Security NSW.

applicable_regulations.name: ISO/IEC 27001 Information Security Management System (ongoing certification)
applicable_regulations.reason: The department's Cyber Security Policy is publicly stated to be a mandatory document for its certified ISO/IEC 27001 ISMS. The department maintains compliance with ISO/IEC 27001 and ISO/IEC 27002. This certification requires periodic internal audits, control testing, and continuous improvement of the ISMS — all directly relevant to secure SDLC and software security controls.

applicable_regulations.name: NSW Audit Office — 'Security of Student Information' Performance Audit (2024-25 active)
applicable_regulations.reason: The NSW Audit Office has an active 2024-25 performance audit specifically examining how effectively schools in NSW ensure student data is secure within their own systems and when provided to third or fourth parties. This audit directly targets the Department of Education NSW and creates immediate compliance scrutiny around software and data security controls.

applicable_regulations.name: Digital NSW Secure Software Development Guidelines (OWASP / Secure Coding)
applicable_regulations.reason: Digital NSW's Digital Service Toolkit explicitly requires NSW Government development teams to align with a secure coding standard, referencing the OWASP Secure Coding Practices Guide throughout the software development cycle. It also mandates automation to reduce human errors in code. This directly maps to Sonar's SAST and quality gate capabilities.

applicable_regulations.name: Canvas/Instructure Third-Party Cybersecurity Incident (May 2026 — active)
applicable_regulations.reason: The NSW Department of Education has confirmed it is actively investigating the impact of the 2026 global Canvas/Instructure LMS data breach, which affected up to 275 million students worldwide and implicated approximately 45 NSW schools. This live incident — involving a third-party software vulnerability — highlights the acute need for software security controls, vulnerability management, and secure SDLC practices across the department's digital ecosystem.

regulatory_impact.summary: The NSW Department of Education is a major government agency subject to mandatory cybersecurity obligations under the NSW Cyber Security Policy (updated February 2024) and the ACSC Essential Eight framework (mandated at Maturity Level 1). The department publicly maintains a certified ISO/IEC 27001 ISMS and must submit annual compliance attestations to Cyber Security NSW by 31 October each year. Digital NSW guidance explicitly mandates secure coding practices (OWASP) and code automation for government development teams. Critically, the department is currently investigating its exposure from the May 2026 global Canvas/Instructure LMS data breach — a live third-party software vulnerability incident affecting approximately 45 NSW schools. Additionally, the NSW Audit Office has an active 2024-25 performance audit specifically examining security of student data across schools and third-party systems. The NSW Audit Office's 2025 Cyber Security Insights report found that 69% of 'Protect' domain mandatory requirements were not fully met across NSW agencies in 2024, and that many agencies failed Essential Eight Maturity Level 1 — creating sector-wide urgency for uplift. Note: APRA does not regulate the Department of Education NSW as it is a public-sector government entity, not an APRA-regulated financial institution.
regulatory_impact.sonar_relevance_reason: The Department of Education NSW faces a high-urgency, multi-layered regulatory and incident-driven case for Sonar. The NSW CSP and Essential Eight mandate vulnerability management, application patching, and application control — all requiring visibility into software vulnerabilities. An active third-party breach (Canvas/Instructure, May 2026) has placed the department under direct scrutiny for software and supply chain security gaps. The NSW Audit Office's active performance audit on 'Security of Student Information' (2024-25) and its 2025 Cyber Security Insights report — finding systemic Essential Eight compliance failures across NSW agencies — create board-level and ministerial pressure to demonstrate security uplift. Digital NSW's own guidance explicitly references OWASP secure coding standards and code automation, which Sonar directly enables through SAST, quality gates, and secure SDLC tooling.
regulatory_impact.recommended_sales_angle: Lead with the live Canvas/Instructure breach: the NSW DoE is actively investigating a third-party software vulnerability incident affecting NSW schools — exactly the type of risk that Sonar's SAST and vulnerability management capabilities are designed to catch earlier in the software supply chain. Reinforce with the NSW Audit Office's active 'Security of Student Information' performance audit (2024-25), which scrutinises how effectively the department secures student data across its own systems and third-party platforms. Anchor compliance value to the NSW CSP Mandatory Requirements: Essential Eight application patching (MR 3.3), application control (MR 3.6), and vulnerability management (MR 1.11) — all of which require evidence of control effectiveness that Sonar's audit-ready quality gates and SAST reporting provide. Reference Digital NSW's explicit OWASP secure coding mandate as the bridge to Sonar's developer-first approach, and highlight that the NSW Audit Office found 59% of agencies lacked independent assurance over their CSP compliance — a gap Sonar's automated code analysis directly closes.

evidence.value: NSW Cyber Security Policy (2023-2024, Version 6.0) — Mandatory for Department of Education NSW
evidence.evidence_text: The department must comply with the NSW Cyber Security Policy to ensure it appropriately manages cyber security risks to its information. The department's cyber security strategy is based on the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and aligns with the NSW Cyber Security Policy and the Australian Cyber Security Centre's Essential Eight. The Audit directorate must conduct systematic and periodic checks to ensure effective implementation of the department's ISMS, including its adherence to the NSW Cyber Security Policy.
evidence.regulator: Cyber Security NSW (Department of Customer Service)

evidence.value: NSW CSP Mandatory Requirements — vulnerability management, application patching, application control (ACSC Essential Eight at ML1)
evidence.evidence_text: The Mandatory Requirements are a minimum baseline for NSW Government agencies to implement, including: 1.11 Establish and maintain vulnerability management processes; 1.12 Ensure cyber security requirements and impacts are assessed as part of change management; 3.3 Patch applications (ACSC Essential Eight); 3.6 Implement application control (ACSC Essential Eight); 3.8 Implement user application hardening (ACSC Essential Eight). These incorporate the ACSC Essential Eight mitigation strategies (Mandatory Requirements 3.3–3.10).
evidence.regulation: NSW Cyber Security Policy — Mandatory Requirements (2024)

evidence.value: NSW Cyber Security Policy Version 6.0 (February 2024) — updated mandatory requirements including 114 detailed requirements across 31 mandatory requirements
evidence.evidence_text: The NSW Cyber Security Policy (Version 6.0, February 2024) sets mandatory requirements for all NSW Government agencies across three domains: Govern and Identify, Detect/Respond/Recover, and Protect. It includes 31 Mandatory Requirements and 114 Detailed Requirements. Annual compliance attestation to Cyber Security NSW is required by 31 October each year. Third-party service provider cyber risk management is explicitly addressed.

evidence.value: ACSC Essential Eight — mandated at Maturity Level 1 for all NSW Government agencies including Department of Education NSW
evidence.evidence_text: The Mandatory Requirements aligned to the Essential Eight maturity level 1 in the NSW Cyber Security Policy are mapped to the controls taken from the December 2024 release of the Information Security Manual (ISM). Agencies must report against these per the Mandatory Requirements. Cyber Security NSW will review changes made on an annual basis for any adjustments to be incorporated for the next reporting period.
evidence.regulation: ACSC Essential Eight (via NSW Cyber Security Policy)
evidence.regulator: Cyber Security NSW / Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC)

evidence.value: NSW Audit Office: Cyber Security Insights 2025 — 69% of 'Protect' domain requirements unmet across NSW agencies; many agencies failed Essential Eight ML1
evidence.evidence_text: The Audit Office of NSW published 'Cyber security insights 2025' analysing NSW CSP compliance data submitted by state agencies to Cyber Security NSW in 2024. Key findings: 69% of 'Protect' mandatory requirements were not fully met by reporting agencies; 152 significant, high and extreme residual cybersecurity risks reported by 27 agencies in FY2024; 59% of reporting agencies did not have independent assurance over their CSP assessment. Many agencies have not met level one Essential Eight cyber protection measures. The number of cyber incidents involving third-party systems nearly tripled in 2024.
evidence.regulation: NSW Cyber Security Policy — Compliance & Audit
evidence.regulator: NSW Audit Office / Cyber Security NSW

evidence.value: Department of Education NSW — certified ISO/IEC 27001 ISMS (ongoing)
evidence.evidence_text: The Cyber security policy is a mandatory document for the department's certified ISO/IEC 27001 ISMS. In developing and implementing cyber security, the department maintains compliance with the NSW Cyber Security Policy, ACSC Essential Eight, ISO/IEC 27001 Information security management systems — Requirements, and ISO/IEC 27002 Information security controls. The department's information security management system enables it to comply with mandatory NSW legislative and regulatory requirements.

evidence.value: Department of Education NSW — information asset protection requirements including PCI DSS for payment processing
evidence.evidence_text: The department's information assets must be protected in the design, development and implementation of its processes and business operations. The policy was updated in 2022 to include the department's requirement to comply with the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard whenever credit card payments are processed (including within schools). The department's ISMS meets ISO/IEC 27001 and ISO/IEC 27002 standards.

evidence.value: NSW Audit Office — Active performance audit: 'Security of Student Information' (2024-25) targeting Department of Education NSW schools
evidence.evidence_text: The NSW Audit Office has an active 2024-25 performance audit titled 'Security of Student Information'. Schools collect and maintain detailed student data, including sensitive personal information. Schools can also require or encourage students, parents and carers to use third party software applications for learning and other school related activities. This audit will consider how effectively schools ensure student data is secure within their own systems and when provided to third or fourth parties.
evidence.regulation: NSW Cyber Security Policy / Privacy and Personal Information Protection Act

evidence.value: Digital NSW — Mandatory OWASP secure coding standard and automated code quality for NSW Government digital services
evidence.evidence_text: NSW Government guidance states: 'Your development team should align practices with a secure coding standard. Teams can use the checklist in the Open Web Application Security Project's Secure Coding Practices Guide (OWASP guide) through their software development cycle. The practices will help mitigate most of the common software vulnerabilities. Where possible, you should automate processes to reduce human errors in code.' NSW Government agencies should use ACSC Essential Eight as the baseline for all digital systems.
evidence.regulation: Digital NSW Secure Digital Services — Secure Coding Requirements

evidence.value: NSW Department of Education — actively investigating May 2026 Canvas/Instructure third-party LMS data breach affecting NSW schools
evidence.evidence_text: The NSW Department of Education is investigating the impact of a third-party data breach that has already compromised school students and staff. The department confirmed: 'The department is aware of the publicly reported data breach affecting Instructure's Canvas platform. The department is working with Instructure to establish if any NSW schools have been impacted and the nature of any data involved.' Approximately 45 NSW schools use Canvas. The department has contacted schools that used Canvas and advised them to reset passwords as a precaution.
evidence.regulation: NSW Cyber Security Policy / Privacy and Personal Information Protection Act 1998 (NSW) — Mandatory Data Breach Notification
evidence.regulator: NSW Information and Privacy Commission / Cyber Security NSW

evidence.value: Canvas/Instructure global breach (May 2026): 275 million student records compromised; NSW DoE and schools confirmed as impacted users; third-party software vulnerability exploited
evidence.evidence_text: Australian universities and education departments are assessing exposure after a breach of Instructure's Canvas LMS compromised personal data of up to 275 million students at over 9,000 institutions worldwide. The NSW Department of Education and WA Department of Education use Canvas for teaching and professional development. The exploit was caused by an issue related to Canvas's Free-For-Teacher accounts — a software vulnerability. Cybersecurity experts warned that 'third-party risk can no longer be treated as a procurement or compliance exercise' and that 'institutional resilience now depends on the security posture of every connected vendor.'
evidence.regulation: NSW Cyber Security Policy MR 1.10 (Third-party risk) / Privacy and Personal Information Protection Act 1998
evidence.regulator: NSW Information and Privacy Commission / Cyber Security NSW / OAIC

evidence.value: NSW Government public sector — mandatory annual CSP compliance attestation and cyber security reporting obligations
evidence.evidence_text: The NSW Cyber Security Policy outlines the Mandatory Requirements to which all NSW Government agencies must adhere to ensure cyber security risks are appropriately managed. By 31 October each year, Cyber Security NSW must be provided with a report for each agency covering an assurance assessment against all Mandatory Requirements, cyber security risks with a residual rating of high or extreme, and a cyber security attestation. DCS-2025-04 mandates expectations for agencies to prioritise uplift to achieve compliance against key Mandatory Requirements.
evidence.regulation: NSW Cyber Security Policy / DCS-2025-04 Targeted Initiatives Directive

evidence.value: NSW audit office finds ‘high risk’ matter in TAFE Commission’s purchasing cards - The Mandarin
evidence.evidence_text: NSW audit office finds ‘high risk’ matter in TAFE Commission’s purchasing cards The Mandarin

limitations: APRA (the assigned regulator) does not regulate the NSW Department of Education. APRA's CPS 234 applies exclusively to APRA-regulated financial entities (banks, insurers, superannuation funds). This assessment has instead identified the correct applicable regulators: Cyber Security NSW, the NSW Audit Office, and Digital NSW.

The NSW Audit Office's 'Security of Student Information' performance audit (2024-25) is listed as 'active' but the final report had not been published as of the date of this research — findings are forthcoming and may create additional compliance urgency.

The Canvas/Instructure breach (May 2026) is an active incident; the full regulatory response and any formal enforcement or findings by the NSW Information and Privacy Commission or OAIC are not yet publicly determined.
The department's ISO/IEC 27001 certification is confirmed by its public policy page as 'ongoing' but the specific certification body and renewal date are not publicly disclosed.

Score capped at 9.0 (within bounds) — the active_fine_lawsuit score of 1.5 (rather than 3.0) reflects that no formal regulatory fine or enforcement action has been issued specifically against the department; the Canvas breach is a live third-party incident under investigation, not a concluded enforcement action.

Department's Data Breach Public Notification Register page — page accessible but limited content returned. Department has a formal breach notification register and mandatory breach reporting obligations.

snapshot.what_they_do: The NSW Department of Education is Australia's largest provider of public education, responsible for delivering and coordinating early childhood, primary, secondary, vocational, adult, migrant, and higher education across New South Wales. It serves two-thirds of the NSW student population and regulates the broader early childhood education sector.
snapshot.who_they_sell_to: Not a commercial entity — serves the NSW public directly. Stakeholders include over 1.2 million learners (K–12 students, early childhood, VET, adult/migrant learners), teachers, parents, and school communities across the state of New South Wales, Australia.
snapshot.regions_scale: Operates exclusively in New South Wales, Australia. The department employs approximately 39,000+ staff and serves over 1.2 million learners. RocketReach estimates revenue in the range of AUD $73B, consistent with its scale as a state government entity funded through public appropriations.
snapshot.business_model: Public sector government department — not a commercial or SaaS business. Funded via NSW Government budget appropriations. Delivers education services free of charge to eligible students. Training Services NSW, a division of the department, also administers vocational education and training (VET) funding and delivery.
snapshot.strategic_direction: The department is executing a multi-horizon Digital Strategy focused on cloud adoption, cyber security uplift, legacy IT modernisation, data governance, and improved digital connectivity for rural and remote schools. It is investing heavily in AI-enabled learning tools and data-driven decision-making as part of its 2022–2025 and beyond digital transformation roadmap.
snapshot.ai_posture: Highly active AI adopter. The department built and deployed NSWEduChat, a bespoke Azure-hosted generative AI tool, rolled out to all staff in Term 4 2024 and subsequently to Year 5–12 students. The tool features privacy-first design, curriculum alignment, built-in content safeguards, and Australian data sovereignty — positioning the department as one of the most proactive government education bodies in GenAI adoption globally.

limitations: This is a government department, not a commercial entity — fields such as 'business_model', 'key_acquisition', and 'revenue' are not directly applicable in a traditional sales context.
Staff headcount (~39,000) and revenue (~AUD $73B) figures sourced from third-party aggregator RocketReach, not the department's own published reports; should be treated as directional estimates.
The department's internal staff portal (Inside the Department) is login-gated and inaccessible to external research.
No acquisitions found, consistent with its nature as a public sector body.

Sonar's typical ICP is commercial software-developing organisations; this account's software/development footprint would be limited to internal tooling, EdTech platforms, and custom tools like NSWEduChat — not a product engineering org in the traditional sense.
